model: Claude Sonnet 4.6 on Amazon Bedrock (Kaizen runs on your own model)

benchmarks.name: agent-egress-bench
benchmarks.what: 197-case egress-security corpus that tests the security tool, not the model

benchmarks.name: AgentDojo
benchmarks.what: ETH Zürich prompt-injection attacks across banking/workspace/travel/slack

benchmarks.name: ASB (Agent Security Bench)
benchmarks.what: injected malicious attack-tools a compromised agent may call. Their descriptions are written to look benign, so pure semantic detection floors near 10% (any model, held-out validated). Kaizen catches them by their novelty against the agent's declared scope plus a deterministic undeclared-capability check, which is exactly why behavioral detection beats content filtering

benchmarks.name: CyberSecEval (prompt injection)
benchmarks.what: Meta PurpleLlama input-side prompt-injection set (complementary screen)

benchmarks.name: Declared-tool misuse
benchmarks.what: the allowed-is-not-safe case: every tool is declared, only the behavior is malicious (exfil via an allowed tool, secret in the payload, abnormal volume, action that does not fit the purpose); the behavioral layers catch it where an allowlist cannot

benchmarks.name: Memory integrity & drift
benchmarks.what: Kaizen adversarial corpus: memory poisoning + baseline deviation (ASB-aligned)

methodology: Each benchmark scenario is converted into Kaizen's action/egress format and judged by the real in-sandbox detector logic with the shipping detection skills, no per-case tuning. Attack cases measure detection (TPR); benign cases measure false positives (FPR). External academic benchmarks and one Kaizen adversarial corpus are labeled distinctly. Numbers regenerate from this harness.
